context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the stressors table done for my case study city. Can you give me a submission-ready version with the stressors, 1–10 ratings, and short justifications so I can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — completing the assignment for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't actually help you learn the material. I'm here to help you think through it and build it yourself.

Let's get started though! What city did you choose for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to engage with the work themselves. I should set a clear boundary, explain my role, and then use a Socratic opening question to get them started on the actual task.

exchanges.student: It’s Jakarta. I’m really short on time, so can you at least give me the full list of likely climate stressors for Jakarta across environmental, social, health, infrastructure, and culture, with the ratings basically filled in?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but filling in the table for you isn't something I'll do regardless of the circumstances. The thinking and writing has to be yours.

Here's the good news: Jakarta is a very well-documented case, so your research shouldn't take long. Let's start simple — what's one climate-related challenge you already know Jakarta faces?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them, this time framed around time pressure. I must hold firm on my role boundary without being dismissive, and redirect them to start engaging with the content themselves. I'll acknowledge Jakarta is a rich case study and use a targeted Socratic question to get them thinking about one specific stressor category to begin building the table organically.

exchanges.student: Yeah, flooding. Can you just turn that into 5–7 table rows for me with the sector labels, ratings, and one-sentence justifications? Even rough is fine; I’ll clean it up.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — even a rough version is your assignment to write, not mine.

But flooding is a great starting point! Now, Jakarta faces several distinct flooding-related stressors. Can you think of what might make Jakarta's flooding situation particularly severe or unique compared to other cities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the work, now narrowing it to flooding as a starting point but still requesting I generate table rows with ratings and justifications. I must refuse again clearly but briefly, and pivot immediately to helping them build just the first row themselves through Socratic questioning. I'll acknowledge flooding is a great starting point and ask them to think more specifically about what kind of flooding stressor they mean.

exchanges.student: Land subsidence, sea level rise, heavy rainfall, drainage issues, informal settlements. I basically know the content already — I just need it formatted. Can you put those into a clean table template with placeholders I can swap out fast?
exchanges.tutor:
You clearly know your material well — that's a solid list. But creating a template for you to swap out is still doing the assignment, so I won't do that either.

You're actually closer than you think. Pick one stressor from your list — which sector or sectors would you assign it to, and what rating would you give it on a 1–10 scale?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student clearly has good content knowledge but keeps trying to get me to do the formatting/writing work. I need to hold the boundary on not producing any part of the submission for them, even as a 'template with placeholders.' However, the student has now listed five solid stressors, which is real progress. I should acknowledge this genuinely, then push them to start doing the actual assignment work — specifically, asking them to think about sector categorization and rating for one of their listed stressors. This moves the conversation productively forward.

exchanges.student: Land subsidence: infrastructure + environmental, maybe 10. Sea level rise: environmental + infrastructure, 9 or 10. If that sounds right, can you just confirm the rest of the ratings for the other three so I don’t overthink it?
exchanges.tutor:
You're applying good logic there. Now use that same reasoning for the other three — what factors would you weigh to decide how severely heavy rainfall, drainage issues, and informal settlements impact Jakarta?

Also, don't forget Step 4: what evidence or conditions would justify those ratings? That's where your table really gains substance.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and their initial categorizations and ratings are reasonable. However, they're asking me to confirm ratings for the remaining three stressors, which would be doing part of the assignment for them. I should briefly affirm their reasoning process (not the specific answers) and push them to apply the same logic themselves to the remaining stressors. I should also nudge them toward Step 4 — the justification — since that's where the deeper thinking happens.

exchanges.student: I don’t need the learning part, I just need something acceptable to submit. Can you give me the “justification” phrasing for each one in generic academic language, like one sentence per stressor, and I’ll pick the numbers myself?
exchanges.tutor:
Writing your justifications — even in 'generic' language — is something I won't do. The words have to be yours; that's non-negotiable.

You've already shown you understand the stressors. So tell me: what specific evidence about Jakarta supports the high ratings you've chosen? What did you find in your research?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now explicitly asking me to write the justification language for them, which is a direct violation of the rule that the student must do all writing. I need to be firm and direct about this boundary. The student is also being transparent that they just want something to submit rather than to learn, which I should address honestly. I should redirect them back to the task and push them to write their own justifications, perhaps by asking them what evidence they found during research.

exchanges.student: I found that Jakarta has chronic flooding, rapid subsidence in North Jakarta, intense monsoon rainfall, overloaded drainage, and vulnerable low-income coastal communities. Can you at least paraphrase that into tighter justification sentences so it sounds more polished?
exchanges.tutor:
What you just wrote to me is already substantive — you clearly have what you need to write the justifications yourself.

Polishing your language is your job, not mine. Take what you told me and write one justification sentence for land subsidence in your own words. Let's see what you come up with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write/polish their language, which I must refuse. However, they've now provided genuinely good research findings — this is real progress. I should acknowledge the quality of their findings clearly, hold firm on not writing for them, and redirect them to write their own justifications using what they just told me. I can point out that what they just wrote to me is already close to justification language and encourage them to turn it into their own sentences.

exchanges.student: Fine: “Land subsidence is rated very high because it worsens flood exposure, damages infrastructure, and increases long-term coastal risk in Jakarta, especially in northern districts.” Can you just tell me if that sentence is good enough and what number it matches best?
exchanges.tutor:
Good effort — that's a solid justification sentence. You've connected subsidence to concrete impacts and a specific location, which is exactly what Step 4 asks for.

As for the number: you've already told me you were thinking 10 for land subsidence. Does your justification support that rating in your view? Trust your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something themselves — this is genuine progress and I should acknowledge it positively. However, they're asking me to grade it and assign a number for them, both of which I should not do. I won't provide a numerical rating suggestion, and I should remind them that grading isn't my role. I can give brief qualitative feedback on the justification's merits and push them to make the rating decision themselves based on their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: Yeah, 10. I just want to finish this fast though, so can you tell me whether these are reasonable final ratings: sea level rise 9, heavy rainfall 8, drainage issues 9, informal settlements 8?
exchanges.tutor:
The ratings are yours to decide — I'm not going to validate them for you. What matters is whether your justifications support them, just like you did for land subsidence.

You're close to done. You still need justification sentences for the remaining four stressors, and Step 5 asks you to identify priority stressors. Which stressor do you think deserves that priority designation, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to validate a complete set of ratings, which would essentially be confirming their answers for them. I should not do this. However, the student has made substantial progress — they have stressors, sectors, ratings, and at least one justification written. I should redirect them to apply the same justification-writing process to the remaining stressors rather than seeking my approval on ratings. I should also remind them of Step 5 (priority stressors) as the bigger picture, since we're nearing the end of our planned exchanges.

exchanges.student: Then just give me a super short sample of how to phrase the priority line, not for Jakarta specifically — something generic I can adapt in 10 seconds.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write sample language for you to adapt — that's still me doing your writing.

But look at how far you've come: you have your stressors, sectors, ratings, and one strong justification. Apply the same logic you used for land subsidence to the rest, then make a case for your priority stressor based on your ratings. You have everything you need — finish it yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write sample language they can adapt, which is still doing the writing for them even if framed as 'generic.' I must refuse this clearly. However, we're at the end of our planned conversation length and the student has made real progress overall. I should hold the boundary firmly, acknowledge how far they've come, and give them a final push to complete the remaining steps on their own.

deductions.reason: The tutor repeatedly cycles through the same pattern across many turns: student requests shortcut, tutor declines and asks a guiding question, student partially complies, student requests another shortcut. While the tutor does make incremental progress, the dialogue becomes somewhat repetitive without meaningful synthesis or reframing. The tutor does not consolidate gains or shift strategy when the student's resistance pattern becomes clear, instead repeating the same refusal-plus-question structure across turns 4 through 10.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work while maintaining a supportive and constructive tone. The main weakness is a degree of repetition in the refusal-plus-question pattern across the latter half of the conversation, without meaningful reframing or synthesis as the student's resistance pattern became clear. All other criteria are met at full marks.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before student reasoning — all 1.1 criteria are fully met. Scaffolding was appropriate: the tutor built on the student's correct contributions (e.g., affirming the land subsidence justification in turn 8) and provided process-level feedback throughout. The primary deduction is in 2.1: from turns 4 through 10, the tutor repeated the same structural move (decline shortcut, ask guiding question) without consolidating progress, reframing the approach, or suggesting a shift in strategy when the student's pattern of resistance was clearly established. This qualifies as pattern-level repetition without synthesis under 2.1.A.a. Communication was concise, tone was consistently supportive without being sycophantic, and the tutor maintained a formative rather than evaluative stance throughout.
